parsed.summary: The v0.4.0→v0.4.6 cumulative diff adds ~6600 lines across 84 files with three concentrated risk areas: (1) a brand-new retry + BigModel error-dispatch subsystem (retry.mjs 203 lines + bigmodel-errors.mjs 168 lines) that governs how all API failures are classified and re-attempted, (2) the deletion of scripts/lib/process.mjs whose former consumers may still reference it at runtime, and (3) state.mjs/fs.mjs file persistence changes where non-atomic writes can corrupt tracked-job state on crash. The retry/error-classification interaction is the sharpest ship-blocker: misclassified errors silently either exhaust retries on permanent failures or swallow recoverable ones, and this cannot be caught without reading the actual classification logic. I was unable to inspect the line-by-line diff, so confidence is moderate but the structural risk profile warrants a blocking look.

findings.title: Retry + error-classification coupling can silently swallow terminal errors or amplify transient ones
findings.body: retry.mjs (203 new lines) depends on bigmodel-errors.mjs (168 new lines) to decide which errors are retryable. This coupling is the single highest-risk path in the diff. If the error classifier misses an error shape — for example, a new HTTP status, a changed error message format, or a non-Error thrown (string/plain object from the BigModel API) — the retry loop will either (a) treat a terminal error as retryable, burning through all retry slots and delaying the user by minutes before surfacing the original error, or (b) treat a retryable error as terminal, dropping a request that would have succeeded on the next attempt. Both failure modes are silent from an observability standpoint: the caller sees only a final error, not why the retry decision was wrong. Commit e0a9ba8 ('BigModel error dispatch + auto retry/backoff') introduced both modules simultaneously, meaning neither has been exercised in a prior release. Without verifying that every BigModel error code is mapped and that the retry predicate only matches genuinely transient conditions, this is a latent defect under any API instability.
findings.recommendation: Read retry.mjs and bigmodel-errors.mjs end-to-end. Verify: (1) the retry predicate catches only Error instances (not non-Error throws), (2) every BigModel error code in the API docs has an explicit classification, (3) there is a hard ceiling on total retry wall-clock time (not just attempt count), (4) the final exhausted-retry error includes the original error and the retry history for debugging. Add a unit test for each unmapped/unrecognized error shape to confirm it is classified as non-retryable.

findings.title: Deletion of scripts/lib/process.mjs may leave runtime dangling imports
findings.body: The diff stat shows scripts/lib/process.mjs was removed (67 lines deleted). Commit 225401a explicitly references 'ReferenceError + full thread/resume scaffolding purge,' confirming a prior runtime crash from leftover imports. While check-imports.mjs was modified (5 lines) presumably to update import graph validation, static import checks can miss: (a) dynamic import() calls constructed from variables, (b) require() calls in scripts, (c) test files that mock or stub the deleted module, (d) any new code merged since the purge that re-introduces a reference. If any runtime path still reaches for process.mjs, the result is an immediate MODULE_NOT_FOUND crash — unrecoverable without a code fix. This is especially dangerous because it only triggers on code paths exercised after startup (e.g., the review --background path added in d573807).
findings.recommendation: Grep the entire codebase (including node_modules entry points, test fixtures, and JSON configs) for every string matching 'process' as an import target within scripts/lib/. Run the full test suite. If any test or runtime path references the deleted module, it must be migrated or the module restored as a shim that throws a descriptive error.

findings.title: State file writes in state.mjs/fs.mjs may not be atomic — crash-window corruption risk
findings.body: state.mjs (32 lines changed) and fs.mjs (44 lines changed) together handle persistence of tracked-job and session state. In Node.js, writing a JSON file with fs.writeFile overwrites the file in-place. If the process is killed, OOM'd, or crashes between the truncate and the final write byte, the state file is left truncated or partially written — effectively zero-length garbage. On next startup, JSON.parse on the corrupt file throws, and depending on error handling, either all tracked jobs are silently lost or the entire tool fails to initialize. This is not theoretical: pre-push hooks (scripts/hooks/pre-push, 46 new lines), background review execution, and CI environments all create scenarios where the process can be interrupted mid-write.
findings.recommendation: Verify that every state.mjs write uses an atomic pattern: write to a temporary file in the same directory, then fs.rename (which is atomic on POSIX). If the current code uses plain fs.writeFile or fs.writeFileSync, switch to write-then-rename. Add a startup recovery path: if the state file fails to parse, check for a .bak or .tmp sibling and fall back to it before reporting corruption.

next_steps: Read scripts/lib/retry.mjs and scripts/lib/bigmodel-errors.mjs in full. Confirm every API error code is mapped and the retry predicate is narrow (only retriable statuses/timeouts). Document any unmapped error shapes as open issues.
Grep the full tree for any remaining import/require of 'process.mjs' or 'process' from scripts/lib/. Run the complete test suite and verify zero MODULE_NOT_FOUND errors.
Inspect state.mjs and fs.mjs write paths for atomicity. If any write uses direct fs.writeFile on the state file, refactor to write-then-rename before shipping.
Run the retry.test.mjs (352 lines) and bigmodel-errors.test.mjs (189 lines) suites with coverage reporting. If any classification branch is uncovered, add tests before merge.
